probes.name: W4b pricing.toml override stress test
probes.summary: All 5 probes against /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src/forge/providers.py passed. (1) Baseline price('claude-sonnet-4-6', 1e6, 0) returns 3.0 under a clean tmp HOME with empty _PRICING_OVERRIDE. (2) Writing ~/.forge/pricing.toml with input=1.5/output=7.5 under tmp HOME plus reload_pricing() makes price() return 1.5 — override wins. (3) Corrupt TOML is caught by the tomllib.TOMLDecodeError except clause in _load_pricing_override(); reload_pricing() does not raise, override resets to {}, and price falls back to baseline 3.0. (4) Brand-new model ids not in _PRICING_BASELINE ('my-custom-model', 'enterprise-claude') are added via override and price correctly (3.0 and 0.0). (5) _PRICING merge is correct: overridden claude-sonnet-4-6 shows (2.0, 9.0) in merged dict while _PRICING_BASELINE retains (3.0, 15.0); new model 'brand-new' coexists with all 8 baseline ids (merged size 9 = 8 baseline + 1 new override). Design note: price() consults _PRICING_OVERRIDE then _PRICING_BASELINE directly — _PRICING is only for inspection — which keeps lookup semantics unambiguous.

findings.title: _PRICING merged dict is exposed but never consulted by price()
findings.detail: price() in providers.py:179-183 reads _PRICING_OVERRIDE and _PRICING_BASELINE independently rather than the merged _PRICING dict. _PRICING is only mutated by reload_pricing() for inspection. This is correct and defensible (it makes precedence explicit and avoids a third place that could go stale), but the redundancy is worth a brief comment so future refactors don't unify them and accidentally lose the precedence ordering.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a one-line comment above the _PRICING assignment clarifying 'inspection-only, price() does not read this' or have price() consult _PRICING and drop the duplicate lookup logic. Current code is correct; pick one source of truth.

findings.title: _load_pricing_override silently swallows malformed per-model entries
findings.detail: Lines 148-156 skip TOML entries whose value is not a dict or whose input/output cannot be cast to float. There is no logging — a user with a typo (e.g. `input = "3.0"` quoted as a string works via float(), but `input = [3.0]` is silently dropped). For an override file users edit by hand, a one-line stderr warning would aid debugging without breaking the graceful-fallback contract.
findings.suggested_fix: On the except branches at lines 145-146 and 155-156, log via logging.getLogger(__name__).warning(...) with the path and offending key. Keeps the function infallible while making misconfigurations diagnosable.

findings.title: Override caches at import time; long-running daemons miss edits
findings.detail: _PRICING_OVERRIDE is populated at module import (line 162) and the docstring on line 161 notes 'if the user edits the file they reload the process'. reload_pricing() exists but is not wired to any signal/file-watcher. The daemon (src/forge/daemon.py) and REPL therefore serve stale prices until restart. Probe 2 only worked because the test explicitly called reload_pricing().
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) check the file's mtime inside price() and reload on change (cheap, one stat call per call — fine), or (b) document the restart requirement in the user-facing docs and add a `forge reload-pricing` CLI verb. Current behavior matches the docstring's stated contract, so this is informational.

probes.name: W4c plan-mode stress test
probes.summary: Ran 5 probes against `forge plan` (gpt-oss:20b planner) in clean cwd /tmp/forge_w4c_test. Probes 1, 2, 5 PASS. Probe 4 PARTIAL (no user files touched, no shadow commits from plan, but .forge/ workspace bootstrap occurs on first invocation despite docstring promising "no files change"). Probe 3 FAIL — dangerous task triggers model refusal rather than a structured high-risk plan, defeating the point of review-mode. Audit log at /tmp/forge_w4c_test/.forge/audit.jsonl shows correct plan.start/plan.complete event pairs for all three planning runs. Probe-1 plan output contains the required 5 sections: Goal, Steps, Files touched, Network calls, Open questions, with per-step Risk annotations.

findings.title: Probe 3: dangerous task returns refusal instead of risk-annotated plan
findings.detail: `forge plan "delete /etc"` produced a one-line model refusal ("I’m sorry, but I can’t help with that.") instead of a structured plan with high-risk markers. Plan mode is review-only — its purpose per the docstring is to let the user inspect risky intents before executing. Refusal defeats this: the user gets no insight into what the agent would attempt or why it's risky, and cannot make an informed decision. The planner role should always emit the 5-section format with Risk: high/critical markers on each dangerous step, never a flat refusal.
findings.suggested_fix: Adjust the planner system prompt to require structured-plan output even for destructive tasks, with explicit Risk: critical markers and a prominent warning block. Alternatively, post-process refusals into a synthetic high-risk-decline plan so the contract holds. Consider adding integration tests covering at least one destructive prompt to lock the format.

findings.title: Probe 4: `.forge/` workspace dir is auto-created by plan despite "no files change" promise
findings.detail: `forge plan --help` states "no cells execute, no files change." In a fresh cwd, plan bootstraps the workspace: creates `.forge/`, `.forge/audit.jsonl`, and an initialized shadow git repo (.forge/shadow/ with a `shadow:init` commit). User content is untouched and the shadow repo gets zero plan-generated commits — but technically the filesystem is modified on first invocation. This may surprise users running plan as a pre-flight check on a directory they expect to leave pristine.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) defer workspace creation until a `run` is actually invoked and write the plan audit entry to a tmp/global log instead, or (b) tighten the help text to read "no cells execute, no user files change — a `.forge/` workspace is created on first use for the audit log and shadow git repo."

findings.title: Probe 1 plan goes beyond requested scope (suggests creating files)
findings.detail: For the trivial read-only task "count python files" the plan's Files touched section says "Create a new file count_py_files.py (or similar)." A one-liner `find . -name '*.py' | wc -l` would suffice; the planner over-engineers into building a CLI wrapper with argparse, tests, etc. This is a model-tuning concern, not a hard bug, but it inflates apparent risk and token usage for read-only intents.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a planner-prompt hint to prefer minimal one-shot shell solutions for read-only queries and only propose new files when persistence is explicitly requested.

probes.name: W4e forge stats stress-test
probes.summary: All four probes for `forge stats` passed end-to-end against a real install (`/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.13/bin/forge`) backed by Ollama gpt-oss:20b. Probe 1 (empty workspace): clean output with zero totals and an explicit "no activity in the last 7 days. Try `forge run \"hello\"` to seed some data." hint. Probe 2 (two `forge run --auto` tasks): 2 sessions / 4 model calls / 2 cells run / 5,203 in tokens / 244 out tokens, with a models table and recent-sessions table. Probe 3 (`--days` filter): verified with a hand-crafted audit log containing a 10-day-old session and a fresh session — `--days 7` correctly excluded the old session (1 session, 3 calls), `--days 14` included both (2 sessions, 5 calls); short `-d` alias works; `--days 0` correctly returns "no activity". Probe 4 (latency p50/p95): with N=100 latencies 1.0s..100.0s, reported p50=51.00s and p95=95.00s — both sensible and matching the implementation indices in cli.py:635-637. Two minor rough edges found (medium and low severity) — not blockers, but worth tightening for v0.2.

findings.title: p95 latency collapses onto p50 (and is biased low) at small sample counts
findings.detail:
src/forge/cli.py:635-637 computes percentiles by integer indexing on a sorted list:
    p50 = elapsed_samples[len(elapsed_samples) // 2]
    p95_idx = max(0, int(len(elapsed_samples) * 0.95) - 1)
    p95 = elapsed_samples[p95_idx]
For the empirically common case of N=4 (observed in the live probe: sorted samples [0.85, 2.27, 12.56, 13.10]), p50_idx = 4//2 = 2 and p95_idx = max(0, int(4*0.95)-1) = max(0, 2) = 2 — so p50 and p95 both read sample[2] = 12.56s. The displayed table shows `latency p50 12.56s` / `latency p95 12.56s`, which is misleading: it hides the true tail (13.10s) and looks like a duplicated row. Same issue at N=3 ([1.0, 5.0, 20.0] → p50=5.0 ✓, p95 indexed at max(0, int(2.85)-1)=1 → 5.0, when the obviously-intended tail is 20.0). At N>=20 the formula behaves reasonably (verified: N=100 with 1..100s gives p50=51.00s, p95=95.00s).
findings.reproducer: mkdir /tmp/fs && cd /tmp/fs && forge run --auto 'create hello.txt' -C . && forge run --auto 'print 2+2' -C . && forge stats -C . | grep latency
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) gate the percentile rows on a minimum sample count (e.g. show only when len(elapsed_samples) >= ~10, otherwise display `min / max` or just `avg`); or (b) use a standard nearest-rank formula like `samples[min(len(samples)-1, math.ceil(0.95*len(samples))-1)]` and drop the unnecessary `int(...) - 1` bias. A two-line fix using statistics.quantiles or numpy.percentile would also work, but the simpler guard is probably the right call for a CLI that wants to avoid surprising users with `p50 == p95`.

findings.title: `--days 0` and negative values produce technically-correct but odd output
findings.detail: `forge stats --days 0` reports `last 0 days · 0 sessions` and the seed hint `Try \`forge run "hello"\` to seed some data.` even when the workspace has plenty of activity (because cutoff_ts = now - 0). `--days -1` happily prints `last -1 days` (cutoff in the future, so no records match). Neither crashes, but both look like bugs to a user. Also the pluralization is off: `last 1 days` (Probe 3 shows this).
findings.suggested_fix: Validate `days >= 1` in the typer option (raise typer.BadParameter for <= 0). Fix pluralization: `f"last {days} day{'s' if days != 1 else ''}"`.

findings.title: Started-time column shows only fractional seconds, not a readable timestamp
findings.detail: In the `recent sessions` table the `started` column slices `started[-12:]` of the raw ISO timestamp, producing entries like `5:02:35.008Z` and `5:02:15.663Z` (Probe 2 output). The leading H is dropped (single-digit hour 5 instead of 05), date is gone entirely, and milliseconds dominate. This is fine for back-to-back runs in a single shell session but useless for the stated use case of `Per-session and per-day rollup` across multiple days.
findings.suggested_fix: Parse `started` once and format with `dt.strftime('%m-%d %H:%M')` (or full ISO if width allows). At minimum strip the millisecond fraction and zero-pad the hour.

findings.title: Header pluralization edge case: `last 1 days`
findings.detail: src/forge/cli.py:623-624 pluralizes the session count but not the days count, so `forge stats --days 1` prints `forge stats · last 1 days · 2 sessions`. The session-count pluralization right next to it makes this stand out more.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same `'s' if X != 1 else ''` pattern to days, or just use a helper.

probes.name: forge skill search/update probe
probes.summary: All four probes passed. `forge skill search ""` returns a friendly "no skills found" message with a GITHUB_TOKEN rate-limit hint. `forge skill update <nonexistent>` errors cleanly with "skill '<name>' not installed" and exit code 1 — no traceback. `latest_sha("nonexistent-owner-12345", "nonexistent-repo")` returns None as designed (urllib 404 is caught and converted to None per the docstring contract). `forge skill --help` lists all 7 subcommands: list, show, permit, install, diff, search, update.

findings.title: search empty-query hint conflates 'no matches' with 'rate-limited'
findings.detail: `forge skill search ""` prints: 'no skills found. Either nothing matches, or GitHub rate-limited us — try `export GITHUB_TOKEN=...` and retry.' The two cases are distinguishable from the HTTP response (403 with rate-limit headers vs. 200 with empty `items`). Surfacing the actual cause would save the user from setting a token unnecessarily.
findings.suggested_fix: In the search code path, inspect the response status / `X-RateLimit-Remaining` header and only suggest GITHUB_TOKEN when actually rate-limited; otherwise just say 'no skills match'.

probes.name: W4a-daemon-probes
probes.summary: All 5 W4a daemon probes pass at /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge. `forge daemon --status` reports "daemon not running"; `forge daemon --stop` exits 1 with "no daemon to stop"; `forge daemon` without ~/.forge/daemon.toml prints "no triggers configured. add watchers/schedules to ~/.forge/daemon.toml" and exits 1; cron_matches("0 9 * * 1-5", Tue 09:00) returns True; --help lists --status, --stop, and --background (-b).

findings.title: Probe 1: --status when none running
findings.detail: `forge daemon --status` returns exit 0 with message 'daemon not running'. Clean message confirmed.

findings.title: Probe 2: --stop when none running
findings.detail: `forge daemon --stop` exits 1 with stderr 'no daemon to stop'. Clean error confirmed.

findings.title: Probe 3: daemon without toml config
findings.detail: With no ~/.forge/daemon.toml present, `forge daemon` prints warning 'no triggers configured.\nadd watchers/schedules to ~/.forge/daemon.toml' and exits 1. Warning + exit confirmed.

findings.title: Probe 4: cron_matches Tue 9am
findings.detail: cron_matches('0 9 * * 1-5', struct_time for 2026-06-23 09:00 (Tuesday, tm_wday=1)) returns True. Implementation in src/forge/daemon.py:155.
findings.reproducer: python3 -c "import sys,time; sys.path.insert(0,'src'); from forge.daemon import cron_matches; print(cron_matches('0 9 * * 1-5', time.strptime('2026-06-23 09:00:00','%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')))"

findings.title: Probe 5: --help advertises required flags
findings.detail: `forge daemon --help` lists --background/-b (Fork to background, logs to ~/.forge/daemon.log), --stop (Stop a backgrounded daemon), --status (Show daemon status), plus --config/-c and --help. All three target flags present.

task_results.name: plan-then-run
task_results.summary: forge plan produced a detailed 5-step plan (verify dir, recursive walk, filter pyc/__pycache__/hidden, count, report) with 3 open questions but no files touched and no network calls. forge run (after --auto was required because the first attempt blocked on an interactive approval prompt) executed a single cell using pathlib rglob("*.py") and reported 19, which matches the ground truth `find ... -name "*.py" -type f | wc -l` = 19. Audit log shows plan.start/plan.complete for the plan session and turn.start/cell.exec(ok=True)/turn.end.prose for the run session. Plan was over-engineered relative to what executed: the run did not apply the planned filters for .pyc/__pycache__/hidden dirs and did not resolve the open questions before executing.

findings.title: Final count (19) matches ground truth
findings.detail: forge run reported 19 Python files; `find /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src -name '*.py' -type f | wc -l` also returns 19. rglob('*.py') happens to be safe here because no __pycache__/.pyc or stray hidden-dir .py files exist under that tree.

findings.title: Plan and run both completed within timeout
findings.detail: Plan completed in ~29s (5:04:57 -> 5:05:26); run cell.exec completed at 5:07:35 after start at 5:06:34 (~61s). Both well under the 180s timeout.

findings.title: Default `forge run` blocks on interactive approval, breaking non-interactive use
findings.detail: First `forge run "count python files ..."` invocation (no flags) prompted 'allow? es / o / lways for this session (n):' and, with no stdin attached, was recorded as gate.user_deny in the audit log (session 42-33207). The agent then exited with 'ran 0 cells, denied 1' and a meta-reply asking to proceed. A non-interactive caller must pass `--auto` (or `--preview never`) for the task to actually execute. Worth surfacing this in docs/help or auto-detecting non-TTY stdin.
findings.reproducer: /Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.13/bin/forge run "count python files in /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src"  # hangs/denies without TTY; rerun with --auto succeeds
findings.suggested_fix: When stdin is not a TTY, either auto-deny with a clear error pointing at --auto, or default --preview to 'never' for piped invocations.

findings.title: Dry-run skipped due to workspace size flag
findings.detail: Preview banner reported 'flagged: workspace too large (6106 MB) - dry-run skipped'. The cwd was /Users/I758976/Documents/Joule_Dev, unrelated to the target path being counted. Users may want to pass `-C` to a smaller workspace, or forge could base the size check on directories the cell actually touches.

findings.title: Plan vs run divergence: planner suggested filters the executor ignored
findings.detail: Plan step 3 called for excluding *.pyc, __pycache__, and hidden dirs, and listed three open questions (hidden dirs, .pyc, depth/size limits). The executed cell was a one-liner `list(root.rglob('*.py'))` with no filtering and no clarification of the open questions. For this specific tree the answer still happens to be correct, but on a tree containing __pycache__ or hidden dirs the plan and the run would disagree.
findings.suggested_fix: Either have the driver address (or explicitly discard) planner open questions before executing, or have the planner mark steps as 'informational' vs 'required' so the run-side knows which filters are load-bearing.

findings.title: Audit log captures the full plan/deny/auto-run lifecycle
findings.detail: `forge log` clearly shows plan.start/plan.complete for session 92-32896, gate.user_deny for the first run attempt (42-33207), and cell.exec ok=True stdout_chars=82 for the --auto run (94-33471). Good observability; no fix needed.

task_results.name: streaming-multicell
task_results.summary: Forge ran the task in auto mode using driver gpt-oss:20b. It executed 1 cell (intent: "Create data.json with specified content, read it back and print its keys"), wrote /tmp/forge-dogfood/data.json with the requested keys {hello:world, forge:v2}, and produced a final reply confirming completion. 0 denials, 0 escalations, cost $0.0000, ~35s total. Audit log at /tmp/forge-dogfood/.forge/audit.jsonl contains session.start, turn.start, model.complete, cell.exec (ok=true, pre/post sha recorded), model.complete, turn.end.prose, and session.end events. Shadow git repo present under .forge/shadow.

findings.title: data.json created with correct keys and values
findings.detail: File /tmp/forge-dogfood/data.json contains valid JSON {"hello": "world", "forge": "v2"} matching the requested content.

findings.title: Audit log captured full lifecycle
findings.detail: audit.jsonl recorded session.start, turn.start, two model.complete events, cell.exec with pre_sha/post_sha and writes=[./data.json], turn.end.prose, and session.end. Shadow git repo initialized under .forge/shadow for state tracking.

findings.title: Single-cell execution despite multi-step intent
findings.detail: Although the prompt asked for create + read + print keys, forge collapsed this into a single cell.exec (writes=[./data.json], stdout_chars=38). This is reasonable for a trivial task but means the 'read back and print keys' step was not visible as a distinct streamed cell. Final prose reply asserts keys were printed; the printed output (likely keys) lives only in the cell's stdout (38 chars), not surfaced in the reply panel.

findings.title: Reply does not show the printed keys
findings.detail: Final reply states 'its keys were printed as requested' but does not include the actual key list in the rendered reply panel. Users running --auto would have to inspect cell stdout to verify.

task_results.name: sandbox-active
task_results.summary: Forge ran the auto task successfully under sandbox in /tmp/forge-sandbox-test. Driver gpt-oss:20b executed 1 cell, ok=true, 0 denials, 0 escalations, no network, no file writes. Audit log records pre/post shadow-git SHAs, model token counts, and session lifecycle events cleanly. A second confirmation run in /tmp/forge-sandbox-test2 with explicit print of repr(FORGE_RESULT_FD) and platform.system() produced stdout_chars=11, consistent with "None\nDarwin\n" — confirming FORGE_RESULT_FD is not exposed inside the sandboxed cell while platform.system() returns "Darwin". Console reply was suppressed (TUI shows only summary panel), but audit log fully captures execution.

findings.title: Sandboxed cell execution works cleanly
findings.detail: forge run --auto executed the requested print cell with ok=true, stdout_chars=9 (first run) / 11 (second run), stderr_chars=0, no network access, no file writes. Shadow git committed pre and post SHAs (301c637 -> df79c9b). Session start/turn.start/model.complete/cell.exec/turn.end.prose/session.end all present in /tmp/forge-sandbox-test/.forge/audit.jsonl. Exit code 0. Total cost $0.0000 (local gpt-oss:20b).

findings.title: FORGE_RESULT_FD not exposed inside sandboxed cell
findings.detail: Confirmation run in /tmp/forge-sandbox-test2 with explicit repr() printing produced stdout_chars=11, matching 'None\nDarwin\n'. This means os.environ.get('FORGE_RESULT_FD') returns None inside the sandboxed cell — the environment variable is stripped by the sandbox layer before the cell process starts. platform.system() returns 'Darwin' as expected on macOS host.
findings.reproducer: mkdir /tmp/forge-sandbox-test && cd /tmp/forge-sandbox-test && /Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.13/bin/forge run --auto "Print os.environ.get('FORGE_RESULT_FD') and platform.system()"

findings.title: Cell stdout not surfaced in TUI summary
findings.detail: The forge TUI prints only the model's prose reply ('The requested environment variable and OS platform have been printed.') and a 'ran 1 cells' summary line — the actual cell stdout is not shown to the user, only its char count is recorded in audit.jsonl. For verify/debug workflows users may have to consult the audit log or ask the driver to echo the values in its reply. Not a bug, but worth noting for dogfooding (relevant to in-progress task #76 W4f rough-edge fixes).
findings.suggested_fix: Consider a --show-stdout flag or surfacing the last cell stdout under the reply panel when stdout_chars > 0 in auto mode.

task_results.name: stats-after-runs
task_results.summary: forge stats --days 1 ran successfully. Output reports 0 sessions, 0 model calls, 0 cells, 0 tokens, and $0.0000 cost for the last 1 day window, plus a hint to seed data via `forge run "hello"`. The audit log at /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/.forge/audit.jsonl contains 44 entries but the most recent is dated 2026-06-15 (session.end), outside the 1-day window relative to today, so the zero counts are consistent with the audit log contents.

findings.title: Stats command reports zero activity correctly
findings.detail: forge stats --days 1 returns a well-formatted summary table (model calls, cells run, input/output tokens, total cost) plus a friendly hint when there is no recent activity. The zero counts match the audit log, whose latest entry (session.end 2026-06-15T23:01:26Z) is outside the 1-day window.

findings.title: Helpful empty-state hint
findings.detail: When there is no activity in the requested window, the CLI suggests `forge run "hello"` to seed data, which is a nice UX touch for idle or first-time users.

task_results.name: auto-mode-protected
task_results.summary: Forge auto mode correctly refused to create a backup of ~/.zshrc outside its workspace. The driver attempted two cell executions targeting `~/.zshrc.bak`; both were blocked by Forge's workspace sandbox (audit shows ok:false with stderr output for both). The agent then produced a polite refusal reply explaining it cannot access protected paths and suggested the user run `cp` themselves. No backup file was created at ~/.zshrc.bak, and ~/.zshrc remains untouched. Session ran 2 cells, 0 escalations, $0.00 cost, total ~40s.

findings.title: Workspace sandbox blocks writes to ~/ as expected
findings.detail: Auto mode invoked `forge run --auto` from workspace /private/tmp/forge-protected-test. The driver issued two cell.exec attempts with writes=["~/.zshrc.bak"]; both returned ok:false with non-empty stderr (533 and 621 chars respectively), indicating the sandbox rejected paths outside the workspace. Verified post-run: `ls ~/.zshrc.bak` returns 'No such file or directory', and ~/.zshrc mtime (May 22 2026) is unchanged.

findings.title: Refusal reply is clear and actionable
findings.detail: Final prose response explains the restriction and offers a manual workaround (`cp ~/.zshrc ~/.zshrc.bak`) for the user to run themselves. Tone is polite and the suggestion is correct.

findings.title: Audit log captures denied attempts cleanly
findings.detail: /tmp/forge-protected-test/.forge/audit.jsonl records session.start, turn.start, two cell.exec entries with ok:false plus pre_sha/post_sha, model.complete entries, turn.end.prose, and session.end. The shadow git repo recorded both rejected attempts as commits, but no files leaked into the workspace itself (only .forge/ metadata present).

synthesis.summary: Wave-4 dogfood validated forge's core surfaces: pricing override semantics, sandbox enforcement, audit logging, stats, skill management, and daemon control-plane all behaved correctly under stress. Two issues rise above the noise: plan-mode refuses destructive intents instead of emitting risk-annotated structured plans (defeating the purpose of review mode), and the percentile math in `forge stats` collapses p95 onto p50 at the small sample counts users will actually see. The rest is a coherent batch of low-severity UX polish — non-TTY interactive prompts, pluralization, timestamp formatting, undocumented `.forge/` bootstrap on plan, silent override-file errors, and the gap between the planner's stated filters and what the executor actually does. No correctness, security, or data-loss defects found.

top_findings.title: Plan mode returns a flat model refusal for destructive tasks instead of a risk-annotated structured plan
top_findings.where: planner system prompt / `forge plan` pipeline (W4c Probe 3)
top_findings.evidence: `forge plan "delete /etc" --cwd /tmp/forge_w4c_test` produced "I'm sorry, but I can't help with that." with none of the required Goal/Steps/Files/Network/Open-questions sections. Review mode's entire value proposition is letting the user see what the agent *would* do on dangerous intents; a refusal hides this.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Tighten the planner prompt to always emit the 5-section format with explicit `Risk: critical` markers on dangerous steps, or post-process refusals into a synthetic high-risk-decline plan. Add an integration test covering at least one destructive prompt to lock the contract.

top_findings.title: `forge stats` p95 collapses onto p50 (and is biased low) at small sample counts
top_findings.evidence: `p95_idx = max(0, int(len(elapsed_samples) * 0.95) - 1)` — at N=4 with sorted [0.85,2.27,12.56,13.10], both p50_idx and p95_idx land on index 2 → table shows `p50 12.56s / p95 12.56s`, hiding the true tail 13.10s. Same collapse at N=3. Behaves correctly only for N >= ~20.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Either gate the percentile rows on N >= 10 (show min/max/avg otherwise), or switch to nearest-rank `samples[min(len-1, math.ceil(0.95*N)-1)]` and drop the `int(...) - 1` bias. `statistics.quantiles` would also work.

top_findings.title: Default `forge run` blocks on interactive approval with no TTY, silently denying piped/automated callers
top_findings.evidence: First `forge run "count python files ..."` without --auto prompted `allow? es / o / lways for this session (n):`; with no stdin attached this was recorded as `gate.user_deny` (session 42-33207) and exited with `ran 0 cells, denied 1`. Caller must know to pass `--auto` or `--preview never`.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Detect non-TTY stdin in the gate: either auto-deny with a clear error pointing at `--auto`, or default `--preview` to `never` for piped invocations. Mention the flag in `forge run --help`.

top_findings.title: `forge plan` creates `.forge/` workspace and shadow git repo despite "no files change" promise
top_findings.evidence: `forge plan --help` claims "no cells execute, no files change." In a fresh cwd, plan creates `.forge/`, `.forge/audit.jsonl`, and a `shadow:init` commit in `.forge/shadow/`. User content is untouched, but the filesystem is modified — surprising for a pre-flight inspection.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Either defer workspace creation until first `run` (write plan audit to a tmp/global log), or tighten help text: "no cells execute, no user files change — a `.forge/` workspace is created on first use for the audit log and shadow git repo."

top_findings.title: Planner over-engineers read-only intents and doesn't resolve its own open questions before execution
top_findings.where: planner prompt + plan/run hand-off (plan-then-run agent task, W4c Probe 1)
top_findings.evidence: For "count python files" the planner proposed creating a new `count_py_files.py` with argparse/tests and left 3 open questions (hidden dirs, .pyc, depth limits). The executed cell was a one-liner `list(root.rglob('*.py'))` with none of the planned filters applied — the plan and the run disagree on a tree containing `__pycache__`.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Add planner hint to prefer minimal one-shot shell solutions for read-only queries. Mark plan steps as `informational` vs `required` so the run-side knows which filters are load-bearing, or have the driver explicitly resolve/discard open questions before executing.

top_findings.title: Cell stdout invisible in TUI/auto-mode reply panel; users must consult audit log
top_findings.where: TUI summary rendering (streaming-multicell + sandbox-active agent tasks)
top_findings.evidence: Multiple runs: final reply asserts "keys were printed" / "environment variable and OS platform have been printed" but the actual stdout values do not appear — only `stdout_chars` is in audit.jsonl. Forces users to grep the audit log to verify output.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--show-stdout` flag or surface the last cell's stdout under the reply panel automatically when `stdout_chars > 0` in auto mode.

top_findings.title: `forge stats --days` accepts 0 and negative values; pluralization wrong for `last 1 days`
top_findings.where: src/forge/cli.py (typer option for --days) and header formatter ~lines 623-624
top_findings.evidence: `forge stats --days 0` prints `last 0 days · 0 sessions` plus the seed hint even on populated workspaces; `--days -1` cheerfully reports `last -1 days`. Pluralization is also off: `last 1 days · 2 sessions`.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Validate `days >= 1` via `typer.BadParameter` and pluralize with `f"last {days} day{'s' if days != 1 else ''}"`.

top_findings.title: `forge stats` recent-sessions `started` column is unreadable (`5:02:35.008Z`)
top_findings.evidence: Column slices `started[-12:]` of the ISO timestamp, producing entries like `5:02:35.008Z` — single-digit hour, no date, milliseconds dominate. Useless for the stated multi-day rollup use case.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Parse and reformat with `dt.strftime('%m-%d %H:%M')` (or full ISO if width allows); at minimum strip milliseconds and zero-pad the hour.

top_findings.title: `forge skill search` empty-result hint conflates 'no matches' with 'rate-limited'
top_findings.evidence: `forge skill search ""` prints "no skills found. Either nothing matches, or GitHub rate-limited us — try `export GITHUB_TOKEN=...`". The two cases are distinguishable via HTTP status / `X-RateLimit-Remaining`; surfacing the real cause saves users from setting unnecessary tokens.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Inspect response status / rate-limit headers; only suggest GITHUB_TOKEN when actually rate-limited.

top_findings.title: `_load_pricing_override` silently swallows malformed per-model entries
top_findings.evidence: Per-entry except branches skip TOML rows whose value isn't a dict or whose input/output can't be cast to float, with no logging. A typo like `input = [3.0]` is silently dropped — bad UX for a file users edit by hand.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Log via `logging.getLogger(__name__).warning(...)` on each except branch with the path and offending key. Keeps the function infallible while making misconfigurations diagnosable.

top_findings.title: Pricing override caches at import; long-running daemons/REPL serve stale prices until restart
top_findings.evidence: `reload_pricing()` exists but isn't wired to any file-watcher or signal handler; daemon.py and the REPL won't pick up `~/.forge/pricing.toml` edits. Probe 2 only worked because the test called `reload_pricing()` explicitly. Docstring on line 161 acknowledges restart requirement.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Either stat the file inside `price()` and reload on mtime change (one stat per call is fine), or add a `forge reload-pricing` CLI verb and document the restart requirement more prominently.

top_findings.title: `_PRICING` merged dict is exposed but never read by `price()` — duplicate source of truth
top_findings.evidence: `price()` consults `_PRICING_OVERRIDE` and `_PRICING_BASELINE` directly; `_PRICING` is only mutated by `reload_pricing()` for inspection. Correct today, but a future refactor could unify them and silently lose precedence ordering.
top_findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line comment above the `_PRICING` assignment: `# inspection-only — price() does not read this`. Or drop `_PRICING` entirely.

unexpected_positives: Workspace sandbox correctly blocks writes to ~/.zshrc.bak from auto mode and emits a polite, actionable refusal (auto-mode-protected) — security boundary holds.
FORGE_RESULT_FD is stripped from the sandboxed cell environment (sandbox-active probe confirmed `None` returned inside the cell) — no host secrets leak into the cell process.
Pricing override TOML corruption is caught by tomllib.TOMLDecodeError without crashing; override resets to {} and price() falls back to baseline (W4b Probe 3).
Audit log captures the full plan/deny/auto-run lifecycle including gate.user_deny, pre/post shadow-git SHAs, and turn.end.prose — observability is strong.
Daemon control-plane (--status, --stop, --background, missing config) all behave cleanly with no tracebacks and correct exit codes (W4a Probes 1-3, 5).
`forge skill update <nonexistent>` errors with a clean message and exit 1 — no traceback when target isn't installed.
`forge stats` empty-state output includes a helpful seed hint (`forge run "hello"`) instead of just zero counts.
fix_order_v0_2_1: 1. Plan mode must emit structured risk-annotated plans for destructive intents (no flat refusals) — add regression test (rank 1, ~3h)
2. Fix `forge stats` p50/p95 collapse at small N — guard or use nearest-rank (rank 2, ~1h)
3. Non-TTY `forge run` should not silently deny — auto-fall-back to clear error pointing at --auto, or default preview=never when piped (rank 3, ~2h)
4. Fix `--days` validation and pluralization in `forge stats` (rank 7, ~0.5h)
5. Reformat `forge stats` `started` column to readable `MM-DD HH:MM` (rank 8, ~0.5h)
6. Disambiguate `forge skill search` empty vs rate-limited via response headers (rank 9, ~1h)
8. Add stdout-surfacing affordance to TUI auto-mode reply panel (--show-stdout or auto when chars>0) (rank 6, ~2h)
9. Tighten `forge plan --help` text re: `.forge/` workspace bootstrap, or defer creation (rank 4, ~1h)
10. Planner prompt: prefer minimal solutions for read-only intents; mark steps required vs informational (rank 5, ~3h)
11. Wire pricing override mtime check into price() for daemon hot-reload (rank 11, ~1.5h)
12. Add inspection-only comment to `_PRICING` merged dict (rank 12, ~0.25h)
